metadata.summary: PRD defines WHAT to build and WHY. Requires: Overview, Problem, Goals, Non-Goals, User Stories, FRs, NFRs, Acceptance Criteria, Out of Scope, Open Questions.

content.title: Product Specification Writing
content.summary: A product specification (PRD) defines WHAT to build and WHY, leaving HOW to design and engineering. Required sections: Overview, Problem, Goals (with current/target metric table), Non-Goals, User Stories (As-a/I-want/So-that with persona IDs), Functional Requirements (FR-N IDs), Non-Functional Requirements, Acceptance Criteria (Given/When/Then per FR), Out of Scope, Open Questions. Every FR must have at least one AC; every goal must be measurable. Spec ends at ~5 pages for most features; beyond that, split or use a Mini-Spec.

why.p: Specs that mix what and how box in engineers and are ignored; specs without acceptance criteria produce untestable scope. Non-Goals explicitly prevent scope creep—their absence is the leading cause of unplanned feature growth. The Open Questions section is mandatory: a spec that has no unknowns signals false confidence and must be questioned before any FR is marked Must.

list.item: Feature is large enough (more than one sprint) that engineers and designers need a shared written contract before work starts.
Cross-team work where multiple stakeholders must approve scope.
Building against an external SLA or paid customer commitment requiring traceable requirements.
Onboarding a contractor—the spec is the briefing artifact.
Tiny changes (less than one day, no ambiguity)—a backlog item with acceptance criteria is sufficient.
Highly exploratory discovery work where requirements change weekly. Spec the experiment, not the product.
After implementation as a retroactive document—that is documentation, not a spec.
When the team will not maintain the spec post-launch; outdated specs mislead more than they help.

rule.statement: A complete spec must contain: Overview, Problem Statement, Goals (with current/target metric table), Non-Goals, User Stories, Functional Requirements (FR-N IDs), Non-Functional Requirements, Acceptance Criteria, Out of Scope, Open Questions. Missing any section is a reject signal.
rule.rationale: Each section prevents a specific class of delivery failure: Non-Goals prevent scope creep, Open Questions prevent false confidence, Acceptance Criteria prevent untestable scope.

rule.statement: Every goal must have a measurable current baseline and a numeric target. "Improve UX" is not a goal. "Reduce time-to-find-product from 3 min to 30 sec" is a goal.
rule.rationale: Unmeasurable goals cannot be verified at launch and cannot drive prioritization decisions during delivery.

rule.statement: List everything explicitly excluded from this spec. Non-Goals section must be non-empty for any feature touching more than one surface.
rule.rationale: The absence of Non-Goals is the leading cause of scope creep. Every unspecified behaviour becomes "obviously included" during implementation.

example.title: Explicit non-goals
example.note: Non-Goals: Advanced filters (separate feature), AI-powered recommendations, Voice search.

rule.statement: Every story uses the format: "As a [named persona], I want to [action] so that [measurable benefit]." Use persona IDs (not "the user") in every story. Include the "so that" clause—it encodes motivation and validates the story is worth building.
rule.rationale: Stories without persona IDs cannot be traced to a validated user segment. Stories without "so that" cannot be evaluated for user value.

rule.statement: Functional requirements use "FR-N: The system shall [do X]" format. Cap at ~15 FRs per spec; larger features must be split. Keep implementation details out: no library names, DB schemas, or UI component names.
rule.rationale: Specs mixing what and how box in engineers and are ignored. Implementation decisions belong in the design document, linked from the spec.

rule.statement: Open Questions section must be non-empty. A spec with zero open questions signals false confidence. List technical unknowns, pending business decisions, and user research gaps with an owner per question.
rule.rationale: LLMs in particular will "answer" every question rather than acknowledge uncertainty. Explicitly requiring a non-empty Open Questions section forces intellectual honesty.

rule.statement: Target 1-3 pages (or ~15 FRs). Use Mini-Spec for features under one sprint. Track decisions made post-approval as a versioned addendum—never silently rewrite the original spec.
rule.rationale: Beyond 5 pages, nobody reads the spec. Silent rewrites destroy the audit trail that later resolves scope disputes.

rule.statement: Assign a unique ID to every FR (FR-1, FR-2, ...) and reference that ID in the corresponding acceptance criteria. An FR without a linked AC is an orphan—reject the spec.
rule.rationale: Without ID-based traceability, FRs and ACs drift apart during revisions. Orphan FRs produce untested scope at launch.

rule.statement: Label every FR with a priority: Must (MVP blocker), Should (high value, deferrable), Could (nice-to-have). Pair with a co-signer for any FR labelled Must.
rule.rationale: Unprioritized requirements produce all-or-nothing scope debates at sprint planning. Must-level FRs need sign-off because they encode strategic decisions.

rule.statement: Every NFR must specify a measurable criterion. "The system shall be fast" is not an NFR. "FR-2 latency: results visible within 200ms at p95 for up to 50K concurrent users" is an NFR.
rule.rationale: Unmeasurable NFRs cannot be verified in QA and become perpetual technical debt claims.

antipattern.description: Generic NFR boilerplate: "99.99% uptime", "GDPR compliant" pasted without source or justification.
antipattern.reason: Generic NFRs fabricated without contractual or regulatory basis waste engineering effort on unnecessary hardening.

rule.statement: Every FR must have at least one Given/When/Then acceptance criterion. Each AC row must reference the FR ID. AC must be unique—never a restatement of the FR.
rule.rationale: AC that restates the FR cannot be used for test case authoring or sprint review verification. "FR-2 search works → AC: search works" tells testers nothing.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A research agent gathers inputs (problem statement, persona notes, analytics, prior art) and emits a structured "context" object. A drafter agent fills the spec template with required sections, never inventing acceptance criteria from thin air. A reviewer agent runs the rubric ("does every FR have an AC? are NFRs measurable? are non-goals listed?"). An open-questions agent extracts unknowns and assigns owners. Humans sign off on the goals/non-goals and on any FR labelled "Must".

list.item: faion-spec-reviewer-agent — primary reviewer to apply the rubric.
faion-task-creator-agent — splits the approved spec into backlog items with acceptance criteria.
faion-mvp-scope-analyzer-agent — feeds in if the spec must be cut to MVP scope.
faion-idea-generator-agent — solution divergence before the spec freezes one approach.
Use IDs (FR-1, NFR-1, US-1) and reference them throughout. Without IDs, traceability collapses on first edit.
Always include Non-Goals; they prevent scope creep more reliably than priority labels.
Cap functional requirements at ~15 per spec. Larger features should be split.
Make every goal measurable with a current/target table. "Improve UX" is not a goal.
Keep "how" out: no library names, no schemas, no UI mocks (mocks live in design doc, linked).
Open Questions section is mandatory; an empty section signals fake confidence.
Specs are versioned: track decisions made post-approval as an addendum, never silently rewrite.
Pair with mvp-scoping if the FR list exceeds capacity—cut to Must/Should/Could before publishing.
LLMs default to elaborate prose; force structured templates with explicit section headings or reject output.
Acceptance criteria written as restatements of FRs ("FR-2: search works → AC: search works"). Require unique G/W/T phrasing per AC.
Models invent NFRs ("99.99% uptime", "GDPR compliant") without basis — strip generic NFR boilerplate unless backed by source.
Open Questions section gets dropped because the model "answers" everything. Tell it explicitly: list things you do NOT know.
Personas conflated with user types (e.g. "the user"). Inject persona ids and require them in every story.
Long context with prior specs: agents copy-paste old FRs without updating IDs, creating duplicates. Reset numbering or reuse existing IDs deliberately.
Specs containing PII / customer names: scrub via pre-publish hook; LLMs do not redact reliably.
Token cost: a typical spec ≈ 4–8k tokens output; embed only the diffed sections in iteration to save context.
Always require human approval on goals + non-goals before any FR work; those decisions encode strategy and cannot be delegated.

section.name: services-and-platforms
section.p: Spec authoring and storage platforms, with agent-friendly API support:

list.item: Notion (SaaS, REST API) — Most flexible, weak structural validation.
Confluence (SaaS, REST API) — Required by many enterprises; heavyweight.
Linear Docs (SaaS, GraphQL API) — Tight coupling with backlog items.
Coda (SaaS, REST API) — Database-backed sections useful for FR/AC tables.
GitHub Markdown in repo (SaaS, Git) — Ideal for solo founders / engineering-led teams.
Productboard (SaaS, REST API) — "Spec" tab linked to features.
Aha! Notebooks (SaaS, REST API) — Strong traceability to OKRs.
HackMD / Outline (SaaS/OSS, REST API) — Lightweight collaborative authoring.

template.purpose: Full PRD template: all required sections with placeholder guidance.
Mini-Spec for small features: one-liner, problem, solution, requirements, exclusions.
